1034	
1035	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1036	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1037	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_germ, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso, PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}
1038	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1039	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1040	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1041	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1042	  \[
1043	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1044	  \]
1045	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1046	  characterised on germs by
1047	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1048	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1049	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1050	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1051	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1052	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1053	
1054	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1055	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
1056	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
1057	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
1058	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1059	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1060	\end{lemma}
1061	
1062	\begin{proof}
1063	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1064	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1065	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1066	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1067	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1068	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1069	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1070	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1071	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1072	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1073	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1074	\end{proof}
1075	
1076	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1077	the load-bearing whiskering-stability field of the abandoned
1078	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route to the
1079	associator (route~(e)). The group law on iso-classes consumes only the
1080	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), which
1081	is now built directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1082	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
1083	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) with no whiskering and no stalks. This lemma ---
1084	including both its PRIMARY and FALLBACK proof routes and the stalk ingredients
1085	(d.1)/(d.2) below --- is therefore off the critical path; its Lean declaration is
1086	being removed, and no future prover should re-attempt it. Retained for the
1087	historical record only.
1088	
1089	\begin{lemma}
1090	
1091	\leanok
1092	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1093	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1094	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
1095	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1096	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1097	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1098	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1099	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1100	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1101	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1102	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1103	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1104	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1105	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1106	  left-whiskered morphism
1107	  \[
1108	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1109	  \]
1110	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1111	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1112	
1113	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1114	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1115	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1116	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1117	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1118	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1119	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1120	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1121	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1122	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1123	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1124	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1125	\end{lemma}
1126	
1127	\begin{proof}
1128	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1129	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1130	
1131	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1132	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1133	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1134	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1135	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1136	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1137	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1138	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1139	  On such a \(V\):
1140	  \begin{itemize}
1141	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1142	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1143	      \[
1144	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1145	      \]
1146	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1147	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1148	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1149	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1150	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1151	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1152	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1153	  \end{itemize}
1154	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1155	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1156	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1157	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1158	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1159	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1160	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1161	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1162	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1163	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1164	
1165	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1166	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1167	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1168	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1169	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1170	
1171	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1172	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1173	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1174	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1175	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1176	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1177	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1178	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1179	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1180	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1181	  \[
1182	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1183	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1184	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1185	  \]
1186	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1187	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1188	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1189	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1190	
1191	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1192	  pinned commit:
1193	  \begin{enumerate}
1194	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1195	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1196	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1197	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1198	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1199	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1200	      above.
1201	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1202	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1203	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1204	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1205	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1206	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1207	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1208	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1209	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1210	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1211	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1212	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1213	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1214	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1215	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1216	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1217	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1218	  \end{enumerate}
1219	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1220	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1221	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1222	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1223	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1224	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1225	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1226	
1227	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1228	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1229	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1230	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1231	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1232	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1233	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1234	\end{proof}
1235	
1236	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
1237	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1238	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1239	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1240	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1241	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1242	formalized; its Lean declarations are being removed.
1243	
1244	\begin{lemma}
1245	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1246	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1247	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
1248	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1249	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1250	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1251	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1252	  \(J.W\):
1253	  \[
1254	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1255	    \qquad
1256	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1257	  \]
1258	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1259	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1260	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1261	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1262	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1263	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1264	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1265	\end{lemma}
1266	
1267	\begin{proof}
1268	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1269	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1270	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1271	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1272	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1273	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1274	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1275	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1276	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1277	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1278	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1279	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1280	\end{proof}
1281	
1282	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1283	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1284	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1285	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1286	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1287	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1288	associator is now built directly by
1289	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1290	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1291	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1293	\begin{lemma}
1294	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1295	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1296	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1297	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1298	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1299	  instance
1300	  \[
1301	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1302	  \]
1303	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1304	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1305	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1306	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1307	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1308	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1309	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1310	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1311	  category
1312	  \[
1313	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1314	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1315	  \]
1316	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1317	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1318	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1319	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1320	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1321	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1322	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1323	  composites.
1324	
1325	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently

1336	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1337	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.

1340	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit

1343	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1344	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1345	  at any point.

1352	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}

1361	  \]

1364	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1365	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1366	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1367	  ever required.
1368	
1369	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1370	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1371	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1372	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1373	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{proof}
1377	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1378	  \leanok
1379	        lem:whisker_of_W, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1380	  \emph{Current realization (as currently formalized).} The isomorphism is at
1381	  present obtained by transporting the canonical \emph{presheaf}-level associator
1382	  \(\alpha\) of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
1383	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1384	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) through sheafification. The substrate tensor
1385	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf tensor, so the nested object
1386	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\) carries an \emph{inner} sheafification (inside the
1387	  left factor) that must be pulled out before \(\alpha\) can be read off. This is
1388	  done with the whiskering-stability lemmas \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\) and
1389	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), which show that the
1390	  sheafification unit, whiskered on either side by a fixed tensor factor, again lies
1391	  in the sheafification localizer \(J.W\); together with
1392	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_sheafification\_map\_of\_W}\) (a \(J.W\)-morphism becomes an
1393	  isomorphism after sheafification) this identifies the two nested sheafified tensors
1394	  with the sheafification of the presheaf-level nesting, where \(\alpha\) applies. The
1395	  associator is then \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\alpha)\) conjugated by these
1396	  comparison isomorphisms. This current route is transitively gated on the open,
1397	  vestigial proof placeholder \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)
1398	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), the load-bearing local-injectivity
1399	  half of the left-whiskering stability \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1400	
1401	  \emph{Planned re-route (target; not yet realized).} Once
1402	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is closed (via the presheaf-level pushforward
1403	  adjunction ``H1''), the associator will be \emph{re-built} by gluing canonical
1404	  local isomorphisms, eliminating all dependence on the whiskering apparatus. Fix a
1405	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1406	  composite
1407	  \[
1408	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1409	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1410	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1411	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1412	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1413	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1414	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1415	  \]
1416	  in which:
1417	  \begin{itemize}
1418	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1419	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1420	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1421	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1422	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1423	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1424	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1425	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1426	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1427	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1428	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1429	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1430	  \end{itemize}
1431	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1432	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1433	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1434	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1435	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1436	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1437	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1438	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1439	  composites agree on the overlap.
1440	
1441	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1442	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1443	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1444	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1445	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1446	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1447	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1448	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1449	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1450	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1451	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1452	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1453	
1454	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1455	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1456	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1457	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1458	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1459	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1460	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1461	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1462	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1463	  specialisation can replace it. After this re-route the vestigial whiskering /
1464	  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1465	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal})
1466	  is deleted; route~(e) survives only as the named last-resort fallback should the H1
1467	  adjunction itself bottom out.
1468	\end{proof}

1641	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1642	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}

1644	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1645	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1646	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1647	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1648	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1649	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1650	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1651	  functor
1652	  \[
1653	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1654	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1655	  \]
1656	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1657	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1658	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1659	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1660	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1661	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1662	  canonical isomorphism
1663	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1664	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1665	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the

1672	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1673	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1674	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1675	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1676	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1677	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1678	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1679	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so

1686	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1687	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1688	
1689	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1690	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1691	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1692	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1693	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1694	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1695	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1696	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1697	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1698	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1699	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1700	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1701	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1702	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1753	  \[

1756	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,

1759	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong

1772	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}

1819	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1820	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1821	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1822	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1823	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1824	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1825	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1826	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1827	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1828	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1829	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1830	  01CR).
1831	
1832	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1833	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1834	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1835	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1836	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1837	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1838	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1839	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1840	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1841	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1842	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1843	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1844	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1845	
1846	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1847	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1848	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1849	  \[
1850	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1851	    \quad
1852	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1853	    \quad
1854	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1855	    \quad
1856	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1857	  \]
1858	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1859	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1860	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1861	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1862	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1863	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1864	
1865	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1866	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1867	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1868	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1869	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1870	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1871	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1872	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1873	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1874	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1875	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1876	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1877	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1878	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1879	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1880	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1881	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1882	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1883	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1884	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1885	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1886	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1887	  pinned commit.
1888	
1889	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1890	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1891	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1892	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1893	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1894	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1895	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1896	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1897	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1898	
1899	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1900	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1901	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1902	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1903	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1904	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1905	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1906	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1907	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1908	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1909	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1910	  already supplies.
1911	\end{lemma}

1913	\begin{proof}
1914	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1915	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1916	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1917	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1918	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
1919	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1920	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
1921	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
1922	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1923	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
1924	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
1925	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
1926	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
1927	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
1928	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
1929	  \[
1930	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
1931	    \quad
1932	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
1933	    \quad
1934	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
1935	    \quad
1936	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
1937	  \]
1938	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
1939	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1940	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1941	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
1942	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
1943	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
1944	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
1945	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
1946	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
1947	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
1948	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
1949	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
1950	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
1951	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
1952	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
1953	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
1954	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
1955	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
1956	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
1957	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
1958	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
1959	  here by hand).
1960	
1961	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
1962	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
1963	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
1964	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
1965	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
1966	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
1967	\end{proof}

1969	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
1970	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
1971	
1972	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
1973	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
1974	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
1975	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1976	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
1977	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
1978	
1979	\begin{theorem}
1980	
1981	\leanok
1982	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
1983	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
1984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
1985	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1986	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1987	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1988	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1989	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1990	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
1991	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
1992	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1993	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1994	
1995	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
1996	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
1997	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
1998	  % groups)
1999	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2000	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2001	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2002	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2003	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2004	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2005	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2006	  % \end{dfn}
2007	  %  [\dots]
2008	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2009	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2010	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2011	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2012	  % topologies  by
2013	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2014	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2015	  %  \end{dfn}"
2016	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2017	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2018	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2019	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2020	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2021	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2022	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2023	  \[
2024	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2025	  \]
2026	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2027	  abelian-group structure with addition
2028	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2029	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2030	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2031	  inverse
2032	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2033	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2034	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2035	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2036	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2037	  map
2038	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2039	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2040	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2041	\end{theorem}
2042	
2043	\begin{proof}
2044	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2045	  \leanok
2046	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2047	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2048	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2049	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2050	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2051	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2052	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2053	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2054	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2055	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2056	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2057	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2058	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2059	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2060	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2061	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2062	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2063	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2064	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2065	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2066	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2067	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2068	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2069	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2070	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2071	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2072	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2073	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2074	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2075	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2076	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2077	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2078	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2079	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2080	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2081	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2082	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2083	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2084	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2085	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2086	
2087	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2088	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2089	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2090	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2091	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2092	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2093	
2094	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2095	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2096	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2097	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2098	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2099	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2100	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2101	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2102	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2103	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2104	\end{proof}
